source: CEFR A1 descriptors + standard reference French grammar (Grammaire progressive du français, CLE International)

cards.title: Greetings & introducing yourself
cards.goal: Can greet people, introduce him/herself and respond to introductions.
grammar: être (présent), y compris questions et négation
exponents: Comment ça va ? — Ça va bien, merci
cards.evidence: Handle a first meeting: greet, introduce self (name, origin, where you live), respond to the other person's introduction, close politely.

cards.title: Exchanging personal information
cards.goal: Can ask for and give personal information: address, nationality, age, family, job.
cards.evidence: Ask five personal questions and answer five, including one about family and one about work; complete a hotel check-in or arrival form.
cards.note: Nationality adjectives agree in gender (il est français / elle est française) — a genuine new concept if the student's L1 has no adjective agreement.

cards.title: My people & my things
cards.goal: Can say what he/she has and talk simply about family and possessions.
cards.evidence: Present three people, three things: introduce three family members or friends and three possessions using avoir and possessives correctly.
cards.note: Possessive choice depends on the gender of the noun possessed, not the possessor's gender (son livre = his or her book) — a common source of confusion.

cards.title: Likes & dislikes
cards.goal: Can say what he/she likes, loves and hates.
grammar: aimer/adorer/détester + nom (avec article défini) ou + infinitif
j'aime LE foot, not j'aime foot
cards.evidence: Six true like/dislike sentences across two topics, at least three with the definite article before the noun object and two with an infinitive.
cards.note: Unlike English, French keeps the definite article before a general noun (j'aime le café) — dropping it is the classic beginner error.

cards.title: Daily life & routines
cards.goal: Can describe habits, routines and daily life in simple sentences.
functions: décrire des habitudes et des routines
grammar: verbes pronominaux de base: se lever, se doucher, se coucher
adverbes de fréquence: toujours, souvent, jamais
cards.evidence: Describe a typical day in six or more sentences, including one reflexive verb and two frequency adverbs.

cards.title: What's happening now
cards.goal: Can say what he/she and others are doing at the moment.
grammar: présent simple as the default for 'now' when no ambiguity
cards.evidence: Picture narration: describe what five people in a busy scene are doing, using être en train de for at least two and plain present for the rest.
cards.note: French has no obligatory present-continuous form like English '-ing' — plain present usually covers 'now'; être en train de adds emphasis rather than being required.

cards.title: Where things are
cards.goal: Can say and ask where things and places are.
grammar: prépositions de lieu: sur, sous, dans, devant, derrière, à côté de, entre
lexis: objets et pièces de la maison
cards.evidence: Describe a room or simple map (five sentences with il y a + place prepositions), then answer two where-is questions.

cards.title: Describing simply
cards.goal: Can describe things, clothes and appearance with basic adjectives.
cards.evidence: Describe three items (one with ce/cette, one with colour + size, one with an intensifier) so the listener can pick them out of a set, with correct adjective agreement.
cards.note: Comparatives are introduced properly at FR-A2-20; here just plant très/vraiment + adjective.

cards.title: What I can do
cards.goal: Can talk about abilities and skills.
cards.evidence: Six true sentences about self and one other person, correctly choosing savoir (e.g. savoir nager, savoir conduire) vs pouvoir (e.g. je peux venir); ask the listener two can-you questions.
cards.note: English 'can' splits into two French verbs: savoir for a learned skill, pouvoir for ability/permission in the moment — a genuinely new distinction to teach explicitly.

cards.title: Numbers & prices
cards.goal: Can understand and use numbers, quantities and prices.
cards.evidence: Ask the cost of three things, understand three spoken prices at varied speed, state two quantities about own life.
cards.note: The vigesimal quirks (70 = 60+10, 80 = 4×20, 90 = 4×20+10) need explicit drilling — no shortcut, just repetition.

cards.title: Time & dates
cards.goal: Can tell the time, understand times and use days/months in arrangements.
grammar: quelle heure est-il ? — il est … heure(s)
cards.evidence: Answer what-time for five clock times; say three true sentences about own schedule using à/en correctly.

cards.title: Finding the way
cards.goal: Can ask for, understand and give simple directions.
exponents: Allez jusqu'au bout de la rue et tournez à droite
cards.evidence: Map task both directions: follow spoken directions to a target, then guide the teacher to a different target.

cards.title: Getting what you want
cards.goal: Can ask for everyday goods and services: order, buy, and make simple requests in shops, cafés and travel situations.
functions: demander des biens et des services
cards.evidence: Two roleplays (e.g. café order + buying a ticket) without L1, including one je-voudrais and one est-ce-que-je-peux-avoir.

cards.title: First steps into the past
cards.goal: Can make simple statements about past events and states.
grammar: passé composé avec être et avoir, verbes très fréquents seulement
cards.evidence: Five true sentences about last weekend or a past holiday: two with être-verbs, three with avoir-verbs. No narrative structure required.
cards.note: Past-participle agreement with être-verbs (elle est allée) is a first-contact detail here; narrative structure is FR-A2-06.

cards.title: First plans
cards.goal: Can say what he/she is going to do.
cards.evidence: Three true going-to sentences about this week plus one going-to question to the listener.

cards.title: Connecting ideas
cards.goal: Can link words and simple sentences with basic connectors.
cards.evidence: Speak or write a postcard with six sentences containing at least two et, two mais, two parce que, all used correctly.
cards.note: This is the CEFR's own A1 coherence criterion.
